proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the 77 binary features, at least one shows a significant association with objective_response (chi-square p < 0.05). Specifically, a sizeable subset of binaries should show absolute response-rate differences of several percentage points between feature=1 and feature=0.

analyses.code: for c in binaries: chi2_contingency(crosstab(df[c], y))
analyses.result_summary: Univariate chi-square screen of all 77 binary features against objective_response. Top hits: feature_013 (rate=15.40% if=1 vs 19.64% if=0; RD=-4.25 pp; p=1.1e-33; n_pos=32365), feature_067 (14.36% vs 17.75%; RD=-3.39 pp; p=2.0e-18; n_pos=12584), feature_006 (18.19% vs 16.38%; RD=+1.82 pp; p=1.04e-6; n_pos=14258), feature_007 (17.54% vs 16.37%; p=5.9e-4), feature_039 (17.39% vs 16.40%; p=3.4e-3), feature_076 (p=0.019), feature_112 (p=0.033). Several other binaries are non-significant.

proposed_hypotheses.text: At least one of the 35 continuous features shows a significant linear association with objective_response in single-predictor logistic regression (p < 0.05). The strongest candidates likely encode disease burden or laboratory markers.

analyses.code: for c in float_cols: Logit(y, add_constant(zscore(df[c]))).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Continuous feature screen via standardized univariate logistic regression. Top hits: feature_011 (beta_std=-0.149, OR per +1 SD = 0.86, p=1.8e-32, mean 3.4 in responders vs 3.9 in non-responders); feature_099 (beta_std=+0.0455, p=1.4e-4, mean 3.82 vs 3.80 — small but real); feature_063 (beta_std=-0.0443, p=6.9e-4, mean 5.78 vs 6.15); feature_092 (p=3.2e-3). All other continuous features non-significant after Bonferroni-style consideration. feature_011 is by far the strongest continuous predictor.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The ordinal feature feature_051 (values 0/1/2) shows a monotonic negative association with objective_response (response rate decreases as feature_051 increases).

Among the four categorical features (feature_057 smoking, feature_043 histology, feature_123 race, feature_005 insurance), at least one shows differences in response rate across categories at p < 0.05.

analyses.result_summary: Ordinal trend test: feature_051 strongly negatively associated with response. Stratum response rates: 0 -> 21.08% (n=17557), 1 -> 15.70% (n=24962), 2 -> 11.05% (n=7481). Ordinal logistic beta_std=-0.254, p=1.7e-93. Other ordinal features (feature_026/033/064/018/045/042) all near-null (smallest p=0.04 for feature_033, no monotonic pattern).

Categorical screen: feature_123 (race) borderline (p=0.045, white 16.55% vs other groups 17.2-18.5%); feature_043 (adeno 16.71% vs squamous 17.37%, p=0.078); feature_057 (smoking p=0.27, current 17.23%, former 16.85%, never 16.38%); feature_005 (insurance p=0.23, medicaid lowest at 16.13%). Demographic and clinical-context categoricals show only weak signal.

proposed_hypotheses.text: When the top univariate hits (feature_011, feature_099, feature_063, feature_051, feature_013, feature_067, feature_006, feature_007, feature_039, feature_076, feature_112) are entered jointly in a multivariable logistic regression, they each retain a statistically independent association with objective_response (p < 0.05) with coefficient signs matching the univariate direction.

analyses.result_summary: Multivariable logistic regression. ORs (per +1 SD for continuous/ordinal, per presence for binary): feature_051 OR=0.77 (p=1.5e-95), feature_013 OR=0.74 (p=1.5e-34), feature_011 OR=0.86 (p=1.7e-33), feature_067 OR=0.77 (p=4.0e-19), feature_006 OR=1.14 (p=6.7e-7), feature_007 OR=1.08 (p=8.1e-4), feature_039 OR=1.08 (p=1.6e-3), feature_099 OR=1.05 (p=1.1e-4), feature_063 OR=0.96 (p=5.2e-4), feature_076 OR=1.10 (p=0.023), feature_112 OR=0.90 (p=0.029). All retain significance with stable direction; correlations among predictors are negligible so they act as independent contributors. Pseudo-R^2 = 0.0197.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the top predictors, at least one pairwise interaction between binary features will be statistically significant on the log-odds scale (p_int < 0.05), reflecting super-additivity or attenuation.

analyses.result_summary: Pairwise interaction screen among 9 top predictors. Three positive interactions detected, all involving the favorable trio: feature_006 x feature_039 (beta_int=+0.184, p=4.2e-4), feature_007 x feature_039 (beta_int=+0.146, p=2.4e-3), feature_006 x feature_007 (beta_int=+0.149, p=4.3e-3). All other pairwise interactions p > 0.08. The three favorable binaries (006, 007, 039) act super-additively.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Defining a combined biomarker score = feature_006 + feature_007 + feature_039 (range 0-3), response rate increases monotonically with the score, with the largest jump occurring at score=3 (all three present), consistent with the positive interactions seen in iteration 5.

analyses.result_summary: Stratified rates by combined score: 0 -> 16.12% (n=9882), 1 -> 16.42% (n=21815), 2 -> 16.79% (n=15087), 3 -> 22.98% (n=3216). Increases are small from 0->1->2 but the jump from 2->3 is +6.2 absolute pp. Trend OR per +1 unit = 1.10, p=1.2e-10. The three binaries are essentially uncorrelated in prevalence (pairwise r ~0.001), so the synergy is on the response not on co-occurrence.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The directions and approximate magnitudes of the top predictors (feature_011, feature_051, feature_013, feature_067, feature_006/007/039, feature_099, feature_063) replicate within both adenocarcinoma and squamous histology subgroups, and their interactions with histology (feature_043) are not statistically significant.

analyses.code: for hist in ['adeno','squamous']: refit; also Logit(y, [pred, hist, pred*hist]).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Stratified models: adenocarcinoma (n=36099, response 16.7%) -> all 9 top predictors significant with same direction; squamous (n=13901, response 17.4%) -> same directions, all significant. Histology x predictor interaction p-values all > 0.22 (e.g., feature_011 p=0.73, feature_051 p=0.22, feature_013 p=0.40, feature_067 p=0.66, feature_006 p=0.62). Effects are histology-invariant.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Race (feature_123) does not modify the effect of the top biomarker/disease-burden predictors; specifically, white vs non-white interactions with feature_011, feature_051, feature_013, feature_006 are non-significant (p_int > 0.05).

analyses.code: Logit(y, [pred, race_white, pred*race_white]).fit() for each pred
analyses.result_summary: Race (white indicator) x predictor interactions all non-significant: feature_011 x white p=0.66, feature_051 x white p=0.84, feature_013 x white p=0.32, feature_006 x white p=0.38. Univariate response rates by race: white 16.55%, hispanic 17.15%, black 17.76%, asian 17.72%, other 18.54%. White patients have slightly lower response but no effect modification.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Smoking status (feature_057: never/former/current) does not modify the effect of the top predictors on response; smoking x predictor interactions are non-significant.

analyses.code: for c in top_preds: Logit(y, [c, never, c*never]) and (c, current, c*current)
analyses.result_summary: All smoking x top-predictor interactions non-significant. feature_011 x never p=0.84, x current p=0.20; feature_051 x never p=0.30, x current p=0.73; feature_013 x never p=0.37, x current p=0.64; feature_006 x never p=0.53, x current p=0.71. Marginal response by smoking: current 17.23%, former 16.85%, never 16.38% (chi-square p=0.27, NS).

proposed_hypotheses.text: Insurance status (feature_005) is not associated with objective response after adjustment for clinical predictors, and marginal differences in response by insurance category are small (<2 percentage points).

analyses.result_summary: Marginal rates: medicaid 16.13% (n=6999), medicare 16.85% (n=21178), private 17.20% (n=19823), uninsured 17.00% (n=2000). Chi-square p=0.23 unadjusted. In the fully adjusted model, all insurance dummies non-significant (smallest p=0.094 for ins_medicaid OR=0.94). Insurance does not contribute meaningfully after adjustment for biomarkers and disease burden.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Age (feature_078, range 30-90, mean ~65) is NOT associated with objective response (logistic p > 0.05), and quartile-stratified response rates are flat (<2 pp spread).

analyses.result_summary: feature_078 univariate: beta_std=+0.0051, p=0.668, NS. Quartile means: Q1 16.51%, Q2 17.48%, Q3 16.65%, Q4 16.94% (range 1 pp). Age is not associated with response in this cohort.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_011 has a strong, monotonic negative dose-response: as the value increases from 0 to >10, response rate falls from ~19% to ~13%.

analyses.result_summary: Binned rates: 0 -> 19.30% (n=14064); (0,1] -> 18.32% (n=3542); (1,2] -> 17.60% (n=3620); (2,3] -> 16.53% (n=3939); (3,5] -> 16.96% (n=7728); (5,10] -> 15.06% (n=13098); (10,30] -> 12.80% (n=4009). Clear monotonic drop, ~6.5 pp from lowest to highest stratum, consistent with feature_011 capturing disease burden (e.g., metastatic site count or similar).

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_051 effect on response is approximately linear on the log-odds scale across its three categories, with each unit-increase associated with a ~25% reduction in odds of response.

analyses.result_summary: Rates: feature_051=0 -> 21.08%, 1 -> 15.70%, 2 -> 11.05%. Pairwise differences are roughly equal on the logit scale (logit deltas: 0->1 ~ -0.34, 1->2 ~ -0.40), consistent with a linear ordinal relationship. Odds-ratio per unit (univariate, on the raw 0/1/2 scale) ~0.66, equivalent to ~34% odds reduction per step. p=1.7e-93. Strongest single predictor in dataset.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_099 is positively associated with response across its quartiles (low values -> low response, high values -> high response), consistent with a favorable laboratory marker (e.g., albumin).
feature_063 is negatively associated with response across its quartiles (high values -> lower response), consistent with an unfavorable laboratory marker (e.g., LDH-like).

analyses.result_summary: feature_099 quartile rates: Q1 (median 3.3) 15.97%, Q2 (3.7) 16.58%, Q3 (4.0) 17.67%, Q4 (4.4) 17.66%. Monotonic positive trend; ~1.7 pp spread. Univariate beta_std=+0.0455, p=1.4e-4.

feature_063 quartile rates: Q1 (median 0.95) 18.06%, Q2 (2.35) 16.80%, Q3 (4.73) 16.71%, Q4 (11.69) 16.00%. Monotonic negative trend; ~2 pp spread. Univariate beta_std=-0.0443, p=6.9e-4. Heavy right-skewed distribution (max 296.88).

proposed_hypotheses.text: The benefit of the favorable biomarker score (feature_006+007+039 = 3) is consistent across severity strata of feature_051, i.e. the absolute response gain from biomarker positivity does not depend on disease severity (interaction p > 0.05).

analyses.result_summary: Stratified rates show consistent ~6-8 pp boost from p3=2 to p3=3 across all severity levels: severity 0: 21.0% -> 27.0%; severity 1: 15.6% -> 21.3%; severity 2: 10.9% -> 18.7%. Severity x biomarker-score interaction beta=+0.010, p=0.41 (NS). The favorable trio's benefit is additive on the log-odds scale and does not depend on baseline severity.

proposed_hypotheses.text: When all top clinical predictors AND demographics (age, histology, smoking, race, insurance) are entered jointly, the demographic variables provide no additional predictive value (all p > 0.05) while the biomarker/disease-burden predictors retain their effects.

analyses.result_summary: Fully adjusted model (N=50000). Significant: feature_011, feature_099, feature_063, feature_051, feature_013, feature_067, feature_006, feature_007, feature_039, feature_076, feature_112 (all p < 0.05). Non-significant demographics: age (p=0.76), squamous histology (p=0.15), smoking dummies (current p=0.65, never p=0.98), race dummies (white p=0.13, black p=0.62, hispanic p=0.31, asian p=0.60), insurance dummies (private p=0.32, medicaid p=0.094, uninsured p=0.76). Pseudo-R^2 = 0.0201; LL=-22253.8.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_013 and feature_067 act independently (no significant interaction); having both present yields the lowest response rate (~13%) compared with neither (~21%), and the joint OR is approximately the product of the individual ORs.

analyses.result_summary: Joint rates: 013=0 & 067=0 -> 20.68% (n=13223); 013=0 & 067=1 -> 16.52% (n=4412); 013=1 & 067=0 -> 16.14% (n=24193); 013=1 & 067=1 -> 13.19% (n=8172). Both-present odds ratio vs neither = 0.58 (~ product of marginal ORs). Interaction beta_int = +0.039, p=0.50 (NS, slight sub-multiplicative trend but not significant). The two negative predictors are independent.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Several second-tier binary features (feature_053, feature_021, feature_074, feature_100) that were borderline in univariate screening become non-significant after adjustment for the top multivariable predictors; feature_076 and feature_112 remain significant.

analyses.code: for cand in candidates: Logit(y, base_X + [cand]).fit()
analyses.result_summary: After adjusting for the 9 main predictors: feature_053 OR=0.96 (p=0.058, borderline), feature_021 OR=0.94 (p=0.059, borderline), feature_074 OR=1.08 (p=0.073, borderline), feature_100 OR=1.16 (p=0.069, borderline), feature_117 OR=0.95 (p=0.22, NS), feature_032 OR=1.04 (p=0.16, NS), feature_038 OR=0.97 (p=0.18, NS), feature_104 OR=1.03 (p=0.36, NS). feature_076 OR=1.10 (p=0.023) and feature_112 OR=0.90 (p=0.029) remain modestly significant. The remaining ~70 binaries appear to be noise.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The negative effect of feature_013 on response is preserved across all three feature_051 severity strata (RD < 0 in each), with absolute risk differences of 3-5 pp.

analyses.result_summary: Stratified rates: severity=0: 013=0 24.19% vs 013=1 19.41% (RD=-4.78 pp, n=17557); severity=1: 18.71% vs 14.05% (RD=-4.67 pp, n=24962); severity=2: 12.20% vs 10.43% (RD=-1.77 pp, n=7481). Effect persists in every stratum, with somewhat smaller absolute difference at the highest severity (where overall rates are low). Direction of effect is uniform.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_006 confers a positive response benefit at all levels of disease burden (feature_011 quartiles), and the benefit does not statistically vary across burden quartiles.

analyses.result_summary: Stratified rates by f11 quartile: Q1: f006=0 18.87% vs f006=1 20.08% (RD=+1.21); Q2: 17.06% vs 19.69% (RD=+2.63); Q3: 16.08% vs 16.97% (RD=+0.88); Q4: 13.47% vs 16.09% (RD=+2.62). Direction positive in all quartiles. Continuous interaction feature_011 x feature_006 beta_int=+0.041, p=0.135 (NS). Benefit holds across burden but is somewhat larger in absolute terms among lower-burden patients.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Combining all favorable predictors yields a 'best' patient profile with markedly higher response than the cohort mean (~17%), while combining all unfavorable predictors yields a 'worst' profile with markedly lower response.

analyses.code: Define best/worst phenotypes via combinations of top predictors
analyses.result_summary: Best-profile patients (feature_011 <=1, feature_051=0, feature_006=feature_007=feature_039=1, feature_013=0, feature_067=0): n=85, response=29.4%. Worst-profile patients (feature_011 >=10, feature_051=2, feature_013=1, feature_067=1): n=102, response=8.8%. ~3.3-fold relative difference between extremes despite small subgroup sizes, indicating clinically meaningful heterogeneity.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_006 (favorable binary) does not significantly interact with feature_011 (continuous burden) on the log-odds scale, i.e. the benefit of feature_006 is not modified by disease burden.

analyses.result_summary: Interaction beta_int=+0.041, p=0.135. Trend toward attenuation at higher burden but not statistically significant. Main effects retained (feature_011 beta=-0.151 NS to inclusion, feature_006 beta=+0.124).

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_006 confers a positive risk-difference benefit within every feature_051 severity stratum (severity 0/1/2), of similar absolute magnitude (~1.5-2 pp).

analyses.code: for s in [0,1,2]: rate(006=1) - rate(006=0) within severity stratum
analyses.result_summary: Severity 0 (n=17557): f006=1 22.17% vs =0 20.62% (RD=+1.55); Severity 1 (n=24962): 17.18% vs 15.07% (RD=+2.10); Severity 2 (n=7481): 12.18% vs 10.59% (RD=+1.59). Direction consistently positive across severity strata. Effect size approximately constant on the absolute scale.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The seven significant binary predictors (feature_013, feature_067, feature_006, feature_007, feature_039, feature_076, feature_112) are pairwise nearly uncorrelated (|r| < 0.05), indicating they encode independent constructs rather than a single underlying factor.

analyses.result_summary: All pairwise Pearson correlations among the 7 top binaries are below 0.01 in magnitude (largest |r|=0.008 between feature_039 and feature_076). The binaries are essentially statistically independent in prevalence; their joint multivariable effects therefore reflect independent contributions to the outcome rather than redundant encoding.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The multivariable model built on the top 9 predictors (feature_011, feature_099, feature_063, feature_051, feature_013, feature_067, feature_006, feature_007, feature_039) generalizes to held-out data with held-out AUC > 0.58, demonstrating that the identified effects represent real signal rather than overfitting in this 50,000-patient cohort.

analyses.code: Random 50/50 split: train Logit on half, predict held-out half, compute AUC
analyses.result_summary: Train AUC=0.599, held-out test AUC=0.604 (similar; slight upward shift due to sampling). The model generalizes essentially perfectly between halves, confirming the top predictors carry true signal. The modest discrimination (AUC ~0.60, pseudo-R^2 ~0.02) reflects the fundamentally stochastic nature of objective response in this cohort -- much variation is unexplained -- but the signal that does exist is robust and reproducible. Final OR summary: feature_051 (severity) OR=0.77 per +1 SD; feature_013 OR=0.74; feature_067 OR=0.77; feature_011 (burden) OR=0.86; feature_063 OR=0.96; feature_099 OR=1.05; feature_006 OR=1.14; feature_007/feature_039 OR=1.08.
